\section{Introduction}


\textbf{File:} Notebook\_BVP\_Plots.ipynb 
\textbackslash\{\}
\textbf{Author:} Elizabeth Gould
\textbackslash\{\}
\textbf{Date:} 10.03.2026
\textbackslash\{\}
\textbf{Problem:} Find the value of \$\textbackslash\{\}Psi\textasciicircum{}\textbackslash\{\}prime\_0\$ which produces equal initial and final values \$\textbackslash\{\}Psi\$

This is my Jupyter notebook for finding values of \$\textbackslash\{\}Psi\textasciicircum{}\textbackslash\{\}prime\_0\$ which solve the BVP of the nonlinear Schr\"{o}dinger equation for persistant current in a single micrometer scale gold loop (\$M\_\{\textbackslash\{\}mu\}\$). 

This file contains several plots and test scripts for solving the BVP of our nonlinear solution. Since our equation for \$\textbackslash\{\}Psi\textasciicircum{}\textbackslash\{\}prime\_0\$ now depends on this value in a non-trivial way, we will procede with a numerical root finding algorithm to find solutions of \$\textbackslash\{\}Psi\textasciicircum{}\textbackslash\{\}prime\_0\$ which solve our BVP.

For our observed approximate solution to the nonlinear Schr\"{o}dinger equation
\textbackslash\{\}begin\{equation\}
\textbackslash\{\}Psi = e\textasciicircum{}\{i M\_\{\textbackslash\{\}mu\} x\} \textbackslash\{\}left( a e\textasciicircum{}\{i k\_\{\textbackslash\{\}mu\} x\} + b e\textasciicircum{}\{- i k\_\{\textbackslash\{\}mu\} x\} \textbackslash\{\}right),
\textbackslash\{\}end\{equation\}
we know that \$\textbackslash\{\}Psi(0) = \textbackslash\{\}Psi(2\textbackslash\{\}pi R)\$ when 
\textbackslash\{\}begin\{equation\}
\textbackslash\{\}Psi\textasciicircum{}\textbackslash\{\}prime\_0 = i M + k \textbackslash\{\}left( \textbackslash\{\}frac\{e\textasciicircum{}\{- 2 i M\textbackslash\{\}pi R\} - \textbackslash\{\}cos\textbackslash\{\}left( 2\textbackslash\{\}pi k R \textbackslash\{\}right)\}\{\textbackslash\{\}sin\textbackslash\{\}left(2\textbackslash\{\}pi k R\textbackslash\{\}right)\}\textbackslash\{\}right),
\textbackslash\{\}end\{equation\}

where \$k\textbackslash\{\}left(\textbackslash\{\}mu, R, B, k, \textbackslash\{\}Psi\textasciicircum{}\textbackslash\{\}prime\_0 \textbackslash\{\}right)\$ and \$M\textbackslash\{\}left(\textbackslash\{\}mu, R, B, k, \textbackslash\{\}Psi\textasciicircum{}\textbackslash\{\}prime\_0 \textbackslash\{\}right)\$ are given by our previously solved model. Due to the \$\textbackslash\{\}Psi\textasciicircum{}\textbackslash\{\}prime\_0\$ dependence of our \$k\$ and \$M\$, we will proceed by moving \$\textbackslash\{\}Psi\textasciicircum{}\textbackslash\{\}prime\_0\$ to the right hand side and solving for the roots of the resulting expression. The resulting 
\textbackslash\{\}begin\{equation\}
F(\textbackslash\{\}Psi\textasciicircum{}\textbackslash\{\}prime\_0) =  \textbackslash\{\}Psi\textasciicircum{}\textbackslash\{\}prime\_0 - i M - k \textbackslash\{\}left( \textbackslash\{\}frac\{e\textasciicircum{}\{- 2 i M\textbackslash\{\}pi R\} - \textbackslash\{\}cos\textbackslash\{\}left( 2\textbackslash\{\}pi k R \textbackslash\{\}right)\}\{\textbackslash\{\}sin\textbackslash\{\}left(2\textbackslash\{\}pi k R\textbackslash\{\}right)\}\textbackslash\{\}right)
\textbackslash\{\}end\{equation\}
can be split into real and imaginary components as follows:
\textbackslash\{\}begin\{equation\}
\textbackslash\{\}mathrm\{Re\}\textbackslash\{\}left(F\textbackslash\{\}left(\textbackslash\{\}Psi\textasciicircum{}\textbackslash\{\}prime\_0\textbackslash\{\}right)\textbackslash\{\}right) =  \textbackslash\{\}mathrm\{Re\}\textbackslash\{\}left(\textbackslash\{\}Psi\textasciicircum{}\textbackslash\{\}prime\_0\textbackslash\{\}right) - k \textbackslash\{\}left( \textbackslash\{\}frac\{\textbackslash\{\}cos\textbackslash\{\}left(2 M\textbackslash\{\}pi R\textbackslash\{\}right) - \textbackslash\{\}cos\textbackslash\{\}left( 2\textbackslash\{\}pi k R \textbackslash\{\}right)\}\{\textbackslash\{\}sin\textbackslash\{\}left(2\textbackslash\{\}pi k R\textbackslash\{\}right)\}\textbackslash\{\}right)
\textbackslash\{\}end\{equation\}

\textbackslash\{\}begin\{equation\}
\textbackslash\{\}mathrm\{Im\}\textbackslash\{\}left(F\textbackslash\{\}left(\textbackslash\{\}Psi\textasciicircum{}\textbackslash\{\}prime\_0\textbackslash\{\}right)\textbackslash\{\}right) = \textbackslash\{\}mathrm\{Im\}\textbackslash\{\}left(\textbackslash\{\}Psi\textasciicircum{}\textbackslash\{\}prime\_0\textbackslash\{\}right) - M + k \textbackslash\{\}left( \textbackslash\{\}frac\{\textbackslash\{\}sin\textbackslash\{\}left(2 M\textbackslash\{\}pi R\textbackslash\{\}right)\}\{\textbackslash\{\}sin\textbackslash\{\}left(2\textbackslash\{\}pi k R\textbackslash\{\}right)\}\textbackslash\{\}right)
\textbackslash\{\}end\{equation\}

Our BVP is solved when this function \$F(\textbackslash\{\}Psi\textasciicircum{}\textbackslash\{\}prime\_0) = 0\$ (expressed either as \$0+0i\$ or \$\textbackslash\{\}left[0,0\textbackslash\{\}right]\$).

\section{Graphs of Solutions to the BVP}

Now for some visualization of my function for BVP matching. We are looking for roots of this function, which takes a complex number and returns a complex number. To do this, we separate the real and imaginary components of both the independent and dependent variables, and solve with a multidimensional root finder. I will then hold either the real or imaginary component of the independent variable (my \$\textbackslash\{\}Psi\textasciicircum{}\textbackslash\{\}prime\_0\$) constant, and plot both components of the dependent variable as separate curves. Our roots occur when the intersection of both functions reaches zero on both graphs. The fact that there are many solutions is visible from the graphs, despite the fact that technically typically only one of them is visible here. This is due to the fact that moving in the fixed dimension moves the graph structure up or down, so any intersection could become a root by adjusting the constant independent variable. Calculating this effect is not straight forward, since the graphs don't necessarily move together, but many (but not all) of the intersections correspond to a root.

Lets start with defining some functions, to reduce the amount of text copied.

\begin{lstlisting}
# Display the numerical results of solving for a root of the given functions numerically.
# - sol0 = the root of the linear BVP matching equation (as a list of two components)
# - fun0 = the linear BVP matching equation which takes only sol0 as an argument and returns something close to [0.0,0.0]
# - sol = the root of the nonlinear BVP matching equation (as a list of two components)
# - fun = the nonlinear BVP matching equation which takes only sol as an argument and returns something close to [0.0,0.0]
def print_results(sol0, fun0, sol, fun):
    print("Linear initial derivative:", sol0)
    print("Function value at this derivative:", fun0(sol0))
    print("Nonlinear initial derivative (for model with error):", sol)
    print("Function value at this derivative:", fun(sol))

# Plots the nonlinear BVP matching equation as a function of the imaginary component of the derivative,
# keeping the real component fixed to the given solution.
# - sol = the root of the nonlinear BVP matching equation (as a list of two components)
# - fun = the nonlinear BVP matching equation which takes only sol as an argument and returns something close to [0.0,0.0]
# optional:
# - nn = the number of points to plot
# - [dgmin, dgmax] is the plot range for x
# - [-lim, lim] is the plot range for y
def plot_fun_vs_imag(sol, fun, nn = 2000, dgmax = 1e11, dgmin = -1e11, lim = 1.5e10):
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    plt.title(f"Plot of the boundary matching criteria vs Im(\u03A8'(0))\n")
    ax.set_ylabel('Value of BVP matching function')
    ax.set_xlabel("Im(\u03A8'(0))")
    
    drline = np.linspace(dgmin,dgmax,nn)
    funline = np.zeros((nn,2))

    for i in range(nn):
        # keep the real term of the derivative constant, plot for imag
        ret1 = fun([sol[0],drline[i]])
        funline[i][0] = ret1[0]
        funline[i][1] = ret1[1]/10

        # remove points outside of our desired range
        if funline[i][0] > lim: funline[i][0] = None
        if funline[i][0] < -lim: funline[i][0] = None
        if funline[i][1] > lim: funline[i][1] = None
        if funline[i][1] < -lim: funline[i][1] = None

    ax.plot(drline, funline)

# Plots the nonlinear BVP matching equation as a function of the real component of the derivative,
# keeping the imaginary component fixed to the given solution.
# - sol = the root of the nonlinear BVP matching equation (as a list of two components)
# - fun = the nonlinear BVP matching equation which takes only sol as an argument and returns something close to [0.0,0.0]
# optional:
# - nn = the number of points to plot
# - [dgmin, dgmax] is the plot range for x
# - [-lim, lim] is the plot range for y
def plot_fun_vs_real(sol, fun, nn = 2000, dgmax = 1e11, dgmin = -1e11, lim = 1.5e10):
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    plt.title(f"Plot of the boundary matching criteria vs Re(\u03A8'(0))\n")
    ax.set_ylabel('Value of BVP matching function')
    ax.set_xlabel("Re(\u03A8'(0))")
    
    drline = np.linspace(dgmin,dgmax,nn)
    funline = np.zeros((nn,2))

    for i in range(nn):
        # keep the real term of the derivative constant, plot for imag
        ret1 = fun([drline[i],sol[1]])
        funline[i][0] = ret1[0]
        funline[i][1] = ret1[1]/10

        # remove points outside of our desired range
        if funline[i][0] > lim: funline[i][0] = None
        if funline[i][0] < -lim: funline[i][0] = None
        if funline[i][1] > lim: funline[i][1] = None
        if funline[i][1] < -lim: funline[i][1] = None

    ax.plot(drline, funline)

# These return the appropriate function handles for the BVP matching equation for the
#  -- linear model
#  -- nonlinear model without error
#  -- nonlinear model with error
# dk, R2, B2, and mu are the parameters as they are passed to the loop class,
# meaning that R2 and B2 are R and B as a percent of their maximum value.
def get_fun_0(dk, R2, B2, mu):
    R  = R2 * R_max
    B  = B2 * B_max
    return lambda x: function_wrapper_bvp(x, mu, dk, B, R, k_calc_f=k_calc_0, M_calc_f=M_calc_0)
def get_fun_true(dk, R2, B2, mu):
    R  = R2 * R_max
    B  = B2 * B_max
    return lambda x: function_wrapper_bvp(x, mu, dk, B, R, k_calc_f=pred_fast_k_true)
def get_fun(dk, R2, B2, mu):
    R  = R2 * R_max
    B  = B2 * B_max
    return lambda x: function_wrapper_bvp(x, mu, dk, B, R)\end{lstlisting}

Now for my analysis. I will start this analysis with my model for \$k\$ and \$M\$ which I got from previous analysis. I know that the \$k\$ model includes an error term, but I will start by trying this analysis without removing the error term. I found that the error term prevents convergence to the linear solution, producing visual results which obviously differ from the correct results.

\begin{lstlisting}
# parameters
dk = 0.5
R2 = 0.8
B2 = 0.5
mu = 1.0e-8

# linear function
yy0 = get_fun_0(dk, R2, B2, mu)

sol = root(yy0, [-1e12, 1e12], method='hybr')
xs = sol.x

# nonlinear function
yy = get_fun(dk, R2, B2, mu)

# scripts for outputing the results
sol = root(yy, xs/10, method='broyden2', tol=1e-20)
dphi0c = sol.x

print_results(xs, yy0, dphi0c, yy)\end{lstlisting}

\textbf{Output:}\begin{verbatim}
Linear initial derivative: [ 2.85673526e+09 -1.06528585e+10]
Function value at this derivative: [0.0, 0.0]
Nonlinear initial derivative (for model with error): [ 3.48037137e+09 -1.02802940e+10]
Function value at this derivative: [0.0, 0.0]

\end{verbatim}

\begin{lstlisting}
plot_fun_vs_imag(dphi0c, yy)
plot_fun_vs_real(dphi0c, yy)\end{lstlisting}

Here is a second set of parameters with the erroneous \$k\$ model, with a smaller \$\textbackslash\{\}mu\$. As can be seen, I don't get results closer to the linear solution.

\begin{lstlisting}
# parameters
dk = 0.5
R2 = 0.8
B2 = 0.5
mu = 1.0e-9

# linear function
yy0 = get_fun_0(dk, R2, B2, mu)

sol = root(yy0, [-1e12, 1e12], method='hybr')
xs = sol.x

# nonlinear function
yy = get_fun(dk, R2, B2, mu)

sol = root(yy, xs/10, method='broyden2', tol=1e-20)
dphi0c = sol.x

# scripts for outputing the results
print_results(xs, yy0, dphi0c, yy)

plot_fun_vs_imag(dphi0c, yy)
plot_fun_vs_real(dphi0c, yy)\end{lstlisting}

Multiple solutions are now visible. Only one of the points of intersection is a solution here, but if I change the other \$\textbackslash\{\}Psi\textasciicircum{}\textbackslash\{\}prime\$ component, I can often move the seen curves up or down until I find another match. This is not guarenteed, but the rings grow in radius as I move away from the origin, providing an infinite number of high energy solutions.

\section{3D Graphs of Solutions}

These are my attempts at trying to produce a 3d plot of the solutions. Something is visible, but they are not the greatest of quality. I stopped trying to improve these as they are unnecessay for further analysis. These plotting scripts tend to run slowly due to the large number of points. I had tried to produce plots with plotly and seaborn instead of matplotlib, but stopped due to time considerations, as better graphics here were unnecessary.

The plots use my function wrapper for the equation, whose roots give the solution to the BVP. I will then wrap this function in another function, which cuts off the values at a given limit. This is done because the values rapidly increase, and if I don't limit them, the relevant part of the function would not be visible on the graphs.

\begin{lstlisting}
# Parameters
dk = 0.5
R2 = 0.9
B2 = 0.5
mu = 1.0e-7

B = B2 * B_max
R = R2 * R_max

lim = 1e11

# Boundary matching function
yy = lambda x: function_wrapper_bvp(x, mu, dk, B, R)#, k_calc_f=pred_fast_k_true)

# Functions to cut off values
def yf2(x, y, lim):
    val = yy([x*kFAu, y*kFAu])
    if val[0] > lim: val[0] = lim
    if val[0] < -lim: val[0] = -lim
    if val[1]/10 > lim: val[1] = lim
    if val[1]/10 < -lim: val[1] = -lim
    return val[0]/1e10, val[1]/1e11

def yf3(x, y, lim):
    val = yy([x*kFAu, y*kFAu])
    valr = val[0]-val[1]/10
    if valr > lim: valr = lim
    if valr < -lim: valr = -lim
    return valr/lim\end{lstlisting}

\begin{lstlisting}
# This code mostly comes from the example for plotting a 3d surface using 
# MatPlotLib. 
fig, ax = plt.subplots(subplot_kw={"projection": "3d"})
plt.title(f"3D plot of the real boundary matching criteria vs \u03A8'(0),\n rescaled to order 1")

# Make data.
sz = 1000 # number of points in each direction
X = np.arange(-1.5, 1.5, 3 / sz)
Y = np.arange(-1.5, 1.5, 3 / sz)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(X, Y)

lim = 1e10 # for which value of our BVP matching function should we cut off
Z1= np.zeros((sz, sz))
Z2 = np.zeros((sz,sz))
for i in range(sz):
    for j in range(sz):
        Z1[i,j], Z2[i,j] = yf2(X[i,j], Y[i,j], lim)

# Plot the surface.
surf = ax.plot_surface(X, Y, Z1, cmap=cm.coolwarm, linewidth=0, antialiased=False)

# Customize the z axis.
ax.set_zlim(-1, 1)
ax.zaxis.set_major_locator(LinearLocator(10))
# A StrMethodFormatter is used automatically
ax.zaxis.set_major_formatter('{x:.02f}')

# Add a color bar which maps values to colors.
fig.colorbar(surf, shrink=0.5, aspect=5)

plt.show()\end{lstlisting}

\textbf{Output:}\begin{verbatim}
<Figure size 640x480 with 2 Axes>
\end{verbatim}

X, Y = np.meshgrid(X, Y)

lim = 1e10 # again, our limit
Z1= np.zeros((sz, sz))
Z2 = np.zeros((sz,sz))
for i in range(sz):
    for j in range(sz):
        Z1[i,j], Z2[i,j] = yf2(X[i,j], Y[i,j], lim)

# Plot the surface.
surf = ax.plot_surface(X, Y, Z1, cmap=cm.coolwarm, linewidth=0, antialiased=False)

# Customize the z axis.
ax.set_zlim(-1, 1)
ax.zaxis.set_major_locator(LinearLocator(10))
# A StrMethodFormatter is used automatically
ax.zaxis.set_major_formatter('{x:.02f}')

# Add a color bar which maps values to colors.
fig.colorbar(surf, shrink=0.5, aspect=5)

plt.show()\end{lstlisting}

\textbf{Output:}\begin{verbatim}
<Figure size 640x480 with 2 Axes>
\end{verbatim}

\section{Runs of BVP matching from the loop class}

From the graphs above, it can be seen that we have a slight problem. There are many solutions to the BVP and they have different current. I have 4 solutions to the problem.
1. Add all the solutions together and calculate the current from this. Strictly speaking the nonlinearity of the solution means that this doesn't quite work (just like adding solutions with different k values or considering that the overall amplitude doesn't matter), but since it is weakly non-linear, this difference may not be relevant. Finding all of the solutions, however, is not such a simple task. There are also likely infinite solutions with rapidly increasing current and amplitude.
2. Use the lowest energy solution. This is most likely the correct solution, and the one we will be using as it is the easiest to find. The solutions with the lowest initial derivative are rare and easy to find as a unique solution. As I increase my initial derivative, the current and amplitude rapidly increase, as does the number of solutions.
3. Use the solution closest to the linear solution. This has the logical benefit of considering continuous diffemorphism from the linear case. I will need to use low \$\textbackslash\{\}mu\$ values for this to work, however. For greater \$\textbackslash\{\}mu\$ values, figuring out which of the solutions is closest to the linear solution is difficult.
4. Varying \$\textbackslash\{\}mu\$ based on \$|A\_\{max\}|\textasciicircum{}2\$. When I started modeling the dependence of \$k\$ on the parameters, I had thought that something like this might be happening, i.e. \$\textbackslash\{\}mu\$ is equal between rings when \$|A\_\{max\}|\textasciicircum{}2\$ are equal, not when \$|A\_0|\textasciicircum{}2\$ are equal. I still think this is logical, but \$\textbackslash\{\}Delta k\$ has a good Taylor expansion for \$|A\_0|\textasciicircum{}2\$, not \$|A\_\{max\}|\textasciicircum{}2\$. This is what I found when fitting the data. Adding this complication also most likely doesn't help fix the problem with multiple solutions. Since it is still unclear as to which variant of \$\textbackslash\{\}mu\$ matching is correct, I will use the easier of the two variants for my analysis. The more difficult one just adds another lengthy root-finding excercise.

My analysis has shown that \$\textbackslash\{\}mu\$ of \$10\textasciicircum{}\{-8\}\$ gives a stable solution as long as the initial values don't grow too much, while still showing a difference in current with the linear solution.

The code below uses my find\_root\_many algorithm within the loop class to find multiple solutions to the BVP.

\begin{lstlisting}
# Here is my script
def test_loop_find_many(R2, B2, dk, mu):
    # Create a loop
    l_calc = loop(R2, B2, dk, mu)

    # Here I suppress warnings, since I know they will occur. Not all of the solutions will work,
    # but I just ignore those and try again with another algorithm or initial guess.
    with warnings.catch_warnings(action="ignore"):
        deriv_found = l_calc.find_root_many()

    # Now I want to print out all of the solutions which I found. I have already thrown away duplicate solutions
    # and any solution which has not converged. Lack of convergence is due to the finding a local minimum, which 
    # has already moved away from the possibility of a root for our values.
    # This structure can be seen in the graphs above, where you can see rings of cases where both functions are 
    # equal. These rings don't always cross zero for all rings and values of parameters.
    print("Exact solution:", l_calc.psi_prime_0())
    print("Exact solution endpoint:", l_calc.psij0(2*pi*R2 * R_max))
    print("Number of found nonlinear solutions:", len(deriv_found))
    for i in deriv_found:
        l_calc.setDeriv(i)
        print('')
        print("Solution:", i)
        print("New solution endpoint:", l_calc.psij_pred_true(2*pi*R2 * R_max), "Absolute value:", np.abs(l_calc.psij_pred_true(2*pi*R2 * R_max)))
        print("New and old current estimates:  ", l_calc.current_alt(),"  ", l_calc.current_old())
        print("New and old solution amplitude:  ", l_calc.amp_max(), "  ", l_calc.amp_max_0())\end{lstlisting}

\begin{lstlisting}
# Loop parameters
R2 = 1.0
B2 = 0.5
dk = 0.7
mu = 1.e-8

# run code
test_loop_find_many(R2, B2, dk, mu)\end{lstlisting}
